name: Codex Alimentarius
description: The Codex Alimentarius, Latin for 'food code,' is a collection of internationally recognized standards, codes of practice, guidelines, and other recommendations relating to foods, food production, and food safety. Established in 1963 by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) of the United Nations, its primary purpose is to protect consumer health and ensure fair practices in the food trade. It serves as a reference point for national food control systems and facilitates international trade in food. However, the Codex Alimentarius has also become the subject of various conspiracy theories. Proponents of these theories often claim that the Codex is a sinister plot by global corporations and governments to control the world's food supply, restrict access to natural health remedies, and ultimately harm human health. They argue that the Codex standards are designed to benefit large agricultural and pharmaceutical companies at the expense of smaller producers and consumers. They raise concerns about restrictions on vitamins, minerals, and herbal supplements, as well as the promotion of genetically modified foods and the use of certain pesticides. Mainstream scientific and regulatory perspectives, however, emphasize that the Codex Alimentarius aims to harmonize food standards globally, promoting food safety and preventing trade barriers. The standards are developed through a transparent and participatory process involving scientists, government representatives, consumer organizations, and industry stakeholders. The WHO and FAO maintain that the Codex standards are based on the best available scientific evidence and are regularly updated to reflect new knowledge and technological advancements. Critics of the conspiracy theories point out the lack of credible evidence supporting the claims of malicious intent and emphasize the benefits of globally harmonized food safety standards for public health and international trade.
subtitle: Global food standards and conspiracy theories

claims.claim: The Codex restricts access to natural health remedies.
claims.evidence: Codex standards on food supplements and nutrient levels.
claims.criticism: Codex aims to ensure safety and quality of supplements, not ban them. National regulations still apply.

claims.claim: The Codex promotes genetically modified foods.
claims.evidence: Codex guidelines on the safety assessment of foods derived from biotechnology.
claims.criticism: Codex provides a framework for assessing the safety of GMOs, but does not mandate their use. It allows countries to make their own decisions based on national policies.

claims.claim: The Codex is a tool for corporate control of the food supply.
claims.evidence: Influence of multinational corporations in Codex standard-setting processes.
claims.criticism: Codex is a multi-stakeholder process involving governments, consumer groups, and industry. Standards are based on scientific evidence and consensus.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Matthias Rath
keyFigures.role: Vocal critic of Codex, promoting alternative health remedies.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Jørgen Schlundt
keyFigures.role: WHO Food Safety Director, advocating for Codex standards.

mythologyConnections: Possible interpretations of food as a source of power and control, analogous to mythical foods like ambrosia.
The idea of a hidden cabal controlling resources echoes themes in various mythologies.

timeline.event: Uruguay Round Agreements of the WTO reference Codex standards.
Ongoing debates and conspiracy theories surrounding Codex Alimentarius.

sources.author: Geoffrey Cannon
sources.work: The Fat of the Land: The Political Ecology of Food
sources.title: Geoffrey Cannon
